steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank begins a new sentence that must end with ‘honors its legacy with his vivid classical portraits of Omeili and her peers.’ The subject is Udé, and ‘honors’ is the main verb. The sentence also contains a long quoted phrase (‘referred to Nollywood…as "Africa's vivid mirror par excellence"’) that describes Udé's prior action. This modifying phrase must be set off with commas and must not create a second independent clause.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Udé has’: ‘Udé has referred to Nollywood…honors its legacy’ creates two main verbs with no conjunction, producing a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘Udé, has’: Same comma-splice problem as A, with a stray comma incorrectly separating subject from verb. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘Udé’: ‘Udé referred to Nollywood…honors its legacy’ still produces two main verbs (past + present) joined only by a comma — a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘Udé, having’: ‘Having referred to…’ is a participial phrase, not a second main clause. Enclosed in commas between the subject and ‘honors,’ it correctly modifies the subject without creating a splice: Udé, having referred…, honors… ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
